refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable operators and the system to achieve and maintain energy efficiency performance such that measured energy_per_ton (energy_used / ton_moved) does not exceed the customer-defined E_target over the agreed evaluation period, under nominal operating conditions and across representative worksite scenarios.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-003 as stated is outcome-focused but lacks specificity on measurement scope, boundary conditions, and system responsibilities. Refinement clarifies that energy efficiency is a measurable KPI dependent on both operator behavior and system design, requires definition of evaluation period and scenarios, and must account for safety-critical constraints that may impose energy trade-offs.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Establish baseline energy consumption profile across representative duty cycles (load classes, terrain, distance, idle time). Measure energy_per_ton in field trials under controlled conditions. Compare against E_target. Decompose variance into system design factors (drivetrain efficiency, auxiliary loads, idle management) and operational factors (operator technique, route planning, load optimization).
sub_frs: FR-3.1: The NGHE solution shall measure and log energy consumption (kWh or equivalent) and work output (ton_moved) with sufficient accuracy and granularity to compute energy_per_ton KPI.
FR-3.2: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time and post-operation energy efficiency feedback to operators and site managers to enable behavior and planning optimization.
FR-3.3: The NGHE solution shall minimize parasitic energy losses in drivetrain, auxiliary systems, and idle modes while maintaining safety-critical system availability.
FR-3.4: The NGHE solution shall support teleoperation and autonomous task planning modes that optimize energy efficiency within safety and operational constraints.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement energy monitoring and feedback subsystem (sensors, data logging, KPI computation) integrated with operator HMI and site management dashboard; optimize drivetrain and auxiliary power management via load-aware control strategies; enable energy-aware task planning in teleoperation and autonomous modes.

DP-B: Deploy real-time energy telemetry collection and cloud-based analytics platform; provide operator coaching and predictive maintenance alerts tied to energy efficiency; implement dynamic power budgeting across vehicle subsystems.

DP-C: Integrate energy efficiency as a constraint in motion planning and task scheduling algorithms; implement regenerative braking and idle shutdown; provide energy cost visibility in operator decision support.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A: Implement a multi-layer energy efficiency architecture comprising: (1) onboard energy monitoring and logging (power sensors, CAN/telemetry integration) feeding real-time KPI computation; (2) operator feedback via HMI (energy gauge, efficiency alerts, per-task energy cost); (3) site manager dashboard (energy_per_ton trending, scenario comparison, anomaly detection); (4) drivetrain and auxiliary power optimization via load-aware control and idle management; (5) energy-aware task planning integration in teleoperation and autonomous modes, with energy efficiency as a soft constraint balanced against safety and productivity.

potential_sources: Energy monitoring accuracy depends on sensor calibration and CAN network reliability (couples to Vehicle electrical architecture and Network Link interface).
Operator feedback effectiveness couples to HMI design and operator training (couples to Teleoperation and Safety Services).
Task planning energy optimization couples to motion planning algorithms and safety constraint enforcement (couples to Vehicle autonomy and Safety Services).
Drivetrain efficiency improvements may conflict with safety-critical braking and stability margins (couples to Safety Services and Vehicle control).
coupling_check.notes: Couplings are manageable via clear interface contracts (Telemetry for sensor data, Tasking for energy-aware planning, SafetyAlert for constraint coordination). No circular dependencies detected. Recommend explicit energy-safety trade-off matrix in design phase.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Energy measurement sensors (power, fuel flow, battery state) are available and can be integrated into Vehicle CAN network with acceptable cost and latency.
ASSUMPTION: Operator behavior and task planning are significant contributors to energy efficiency; system design alone cannot achieve E_target without operational optimization.
RISK: Energy monitoring adds computational and communication overhead; must not degrade real-time safety-critical system performance.
RISK: Energy-aware task planning may recommend strategies (e.g., reduced speed, longer routes) that conflict with productivity or safety margins; requires careful constraint prioritization.
RISK: Evaluation period and scenarios must be representative; cherry-picked conditions could mask poor efficiency in real-world use.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-A to explicitly separate energy monitoring (passive telemetry) from energy optimization (active control). Clarify that energy efficiency is a system-level KPI influenced by design, operations, and maintenance; no single DP component can guarantee E_target. Recommend phased implementation: Phase 1 (monitoring and feedback), Phase 2 (drivetrain optimization), Phase 3 (task planning integration).

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution IM is the top-level system boundary encompassing all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) and their integration. Energy efficiency KPI (energy_per_ton) is a system-level property that emerges from the interaction of vehicle design, operational modes, and safety constraints. The NGHE Solution IM owns the energy efficiency acceptance criteria, measurement methodology, and cross-subsystem optimization.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Energy consumption data (power draw, fuel/battery usage, work output metrics) streamed from Vehicle to external monitoring systems (site manager dashboard, cloud analytics). Frequency: 1 Hz or higher for real-time feedback; aggregated summaries for post-operation analysis.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Task definitions and energy efficiency preferences from site manager or autonomous planner. Includes load class, destination, priority (productivity vs. energy), and energy budget constraints. Enables energy-aware task planning.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional safety constraint signaling. Energy optimization strategies must not violate safety margins (e.g., reduced braking capacity, stability limits). Safety Services signals constraints; NGHE Solution acknowledges and adjusts energy optimization accordingly.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communication backbone for telemetry uplink, tasking downlink, and safety alert exchange. Energy efficiency monitoring and teleoperation depend on reliable network connectivity.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural top-level owner of energy efficiency KPI. All sub-components (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) contribute to the KPI. Minimal diffusion: energy monitoring logic is localized; energy optimization is distributed across Vehicle (drivetrain control) and Teleoperation (task planning), but coordination is managed via well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert). Ownership is clear.

Vehicle owns drivetrain efficiency, auxiliary power management, and onboard energy monitoring sensors. However, energy_per_ton KPI also depends on operator behavior (Teleoperation) and safety constraints (Safety Services). If energy efficiency were assigned solely to Vehicle, it would diffuse responsibility for KPI achievement and create coupling to external factors. Vehicle is a critical contributor but not the sole owner.

Teleoperation owns operator feedback (HMI energy gauge, efficiency alerts) and energy-aware task planning. However, energy efficiency also depends on Vehicle design and Safety Services constraints. Assigning KPI ownership to Teleoperation would obscure Vehicle design responsibilities and create false coupling to operator skill. Teleoperation is a significant contributor but not the sole owner.

Safety Services owns safety constraint enforcement, which indirectly affects energy efficiency (e.g., safety margins may require higher power reserves, reducing efficiency). However, Safety Services does not own energy monitoring, optimization, or KPI measurement. Assigning energy efficiency KPI to Safety Services would create high diffusion and confusion of concerns. Safety Services is a constraint provider, not the owner.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Energy efficiency (energy_per_ton KPI) is a system-level emergent property that cannot be localized to a single subsystem. The NGHE Solution IM is the appropriate owner because it: (1) defines the acceptance criteria and measurement methodology; (2) integrates contributions from Vehicle (design efficiency), Teleoperation (operational optimization), and Safety Services (constraint coordination); (3) manages trade-offs and cross-subsystem optimization via well-defined interfaces. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services are allocated specific sub-requirements (FR-3.1 through FR-3.4) but report energy efficiency performance to NGHE Solution for KPI aggregation.
containment_risks: RISK: If energy monitoring is implemented only in Vehicle without integration into Teleoperation feedback and Safety Services constraint checking, the KPI will be measured but not actionable. Mitigation: Ensure Telemetry interface is bidirectional and consumed by all subsystems.

RISK: Energy-aware task planning (FR-3.4) may be assigned to Teleoperation, but Vehicle drivetrain optimization (FR-3.3) may be assigned to Vehicle. If these are not coordinated, energy efficiency gains may be suboptimal. Mitigation: Establish energy optimization trade-off matrix and cross-subsystem design reviews.

RISK: Safety constraints (Safety Services) may impose energy penalties (e.g., higher idle power for safety systems). If not explicitly tracked, energy_per_ton may appear to fail E_target even if design is optimal. Mitigation: Decompose energy_per_ton into design efficiency and safety overhead; track separately.

RISK: Evaluation period and scenarios must be agreed with customer and representative of real-world use. If not, KPI measurement may not reflect actual field performance. Mitigation: Establish evaluation protocol as part of acceptance criteria definition.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Assign energy efficiency KPI ownership to NGHE Solution. Allocate sub-requirements to Vehicle (FR-3.1, FR-3.3), Teleoperation (FR-3.2, FR-3.4), and Safety Services (FR-3.4 constraint coordination). Establish energy efficiency working group with representatives from all subsystems to manage trade-offs and cross-subsystem optimization. Define Telemetry, Tasking, and SafetyAlert interface contracts explicitly to ensure energy data flows and constraints are communicated. Recommend phased implementation with energy monitoring (Phase 1) as foundation for optimization (Phases 2-3).

fr_dp_gate.reason: Refined FR-3 (energy efficiency KPI) is clear, measurable, and traceable to customer acceptance criteria (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target). Selected DP-A provides a comprehensive, multi-layer approach to energy monitoring, feedback, and optimization that directly addresses the FR. DP-A is not over-constrained and allows flexibility in implementation (e.g., phased rollout, algorithm selection). No circular logic or missing functional gaps detected. FR-DP mapping is sound.

diffusion_risks: Energy monitoring logic is distributed across Vehicle (sensors, CAN integration), Teleoperation (HMI feedback), and NGHE Solution (KPI computation and dashboard). Mitigation: Establish clear data flow contracts via Telemetry interface; centralize KPI computation logic in NGHE Solution.

Energy optimization is distributed across Vehicle (drivetrain control), Teleoperation (task planning), and Safety Services (constraint enforcement). Mitigation: Establish energy-safety trade-off matrix and cross-subsystem design reviews; use SafetyAlert interface for constraint coordination.

Operator feedback (FR-3.2) is owned by Teleoperation but depends on energy data from Vehicle and KPI computation from NGHE Solution. Mitigation: Ensure Telemetry interface provides real-time energy data with sufficient latency and accuracy for HMI display.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate top-level owner of energy efficiency KPI. Sub-components (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) are allocated specific sub-requirements but coordinate via well-defined interfaces. No single subsystem is overloaded with energy efficiency responsibility. Containment is acceptable with explicit interface contracts and cross-subsystem coordination.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Energy efficiency KPI (energy_per_ton) is measurable via onboard sensors (power, fuel/battery, work output) and post-operation analysis. Verification requires: (1) sensor calibration and validation; (2) field trials under representative duty cycles; (3) comparison against E_target over agreed evaluation period. Feasibility is high if evaluation protocol is established upfront and scenarios are representative.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR-3 is decomposed into four sub-FRs (FR-3.1 through FR-3.4) with clear allocation to subsystems: FR-3.1 (monitoring) to Vehicle, FR-3.2 (feedback) to Teleoperation, FR-3.3 (optimization) to Vehicle and Safety Services, FR-3.4 (task planning) to Teleoperation. Each sub-FR is traceable to DP-A components. Allocation is clear and non-overlapping.

recommended_revisions: Establish energy efficiency evaluation protocol (duty cycles, scenarios, measurement methodology, E_target definition) as part of acceptance criteria. Document in system specification.

Define Telemetry, Tasking, and SafetyAlert interface contracts explicitly, including data types, frequencies, latencies, and error handling. Ensure energy data flows are bidirectional and consumed by all relevant subsystems.
Create energy-safety trade-off matrix to guide design decisions when energy optimization conflicts with safety constraints. Escalate conflicts to system engineer and project manager.

Recommend phased implementation: Phase 1 (energy monitoring and feedback), Phase 2 (drivetrain optimization), Phase 3 (task planning integration). Allows early validation of monitoring accuracy before optimization.
Establish cross-subsystem energy efficiency working group with representatives from Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services, and NGHE Solution to manage trade-offs and coordinate optimization.

decision.justification: Refined FR-3 is clear, measurable, and decomposed into four atomic sub-FRs (FR-3.1 through FR-3.4). Selected DP-A is comprehensive and addresses all sub-FRs. NGHE Solution is the appropriate top-level owner of energy efficiency KPI, with clear allocation to subsystems via well-defined interfaces. No blocking issues detected at current level. Recommend proceeding to next decomposition level to detail sub-FRs (FR-3.1 through FR-3.4) and allocate to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Energy efficiency is a high-priority KPI with safety criticality; detailed design and cross-subsystem coordination are essential.
decision.justification_detail: FR-3 is not atomic (is_atomic: false) and requires further decomposition. Sub-FRs are well-scoped and ready for next-level design. DP-A provides sufficient guidance for subsystem-level DPs. IM assignment to NGHE Solution is sound with clear interface contracts. No revisions needed at current level; proceed to decompose sub-FRs.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-3.1: The NGHE solution shall measure and log energy consumption (kWh or equivalent) and work output (ton_moved) with sufficient accuracy and granularity to compute energy_per_ton KPI.
FR-3.2: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time and post-operation energy efficiency feedback to operators and site managers to enable behavior and planning optimization.
FR-3.3: The NGHE solution shall minimize parasitic energy losses in drivetrain, auxiliary systems, and idle modes while maintaining safety-critical system availability.
FR-3.4: The NGHE solution shall support teleoperation and autonomous task planning modes that optimize energy efficiency within safety and operational constraints.

assumptions: Energy measurement sensors (power, fuel flow, battery state) are available and can be integrated into Vehicle CAN network with acceptable cost and latency.
Operator behavior and task planning are significant contributors to energy efficiency; system design alone cannot achieve E_target without operational optimization.
Evaluation period and scenarios are representative of real-world use and agreed with customer upfront.
Safety constraints (Safety Services) are well-defined and can be communicated via SafetyAlert interface to enable energy-safety trade-off analysis.
Energy efficiency is a soft constraint that can be balanced against productivity and safety; no hard energy budget is imposed that would conflict with safety margins.
open_risks: Energy monitoring adds computational and communication overhead; must not degrade real-time safety-critical system performance. Mitigation: Conduct latency and CPU load analysis during detailed design.

Energy-aware task planning may recommend strategies (e.g., reduced speed, longer routes) that conflict with productivity or safety margins. Mitigation: Establish energy-safety trade-off matrix and operator override mechanisms.

Evaluation period and scenarios must be representative; cherry-picked conditions could mask poor efficiency in real-world use. Mitigation: Establish evaluation protocol with customer and independent validation.
Drivetrain efficiency improvements may conflict with safety-critical braking and stability margins. Mitigation: Conduct safety analysis and establish energy-safety design envelope.

Operator training and adoption are critical for energy efficiency gains; without proper feedback and incentives, operators may not optimize behavior. Mitigation: Develop operator training program and gamification features in HMI.
evidence_needed: Baseline energy consumption profile across representative duty cycles (load classes, terrain, distance, idle time).
Sensor specifications and calibration procedures for power, fuel/battery, and work output measurement.
Field trial results comparing energy_per_ton against E_target under controlled conditions.
Operator feedback on HMI energy gauge and efficiency alerts (usability, actionability).
Safety analysis documenting energy-safety trade-offs and design envelope.
Cross-subsystem interface specifications (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert) with data types, frequencies, and error handling.
Energy efficiency working group meeting notes documenting trade-off decisions and design rationale.
